title: Shimashki

maps.text: Part of map of Asia highlighting the region from Mesopotamia to the Zagros Mountains in Iran, representing the areas occupied by the ancient Sumerians (Kessler, P. L., , Map of Sumer). The area is marked approximately to provide the reader with a clearer visualization

common.text:
### Introduction

Shimashki, like the city of Awan, are localities that, despite not yet having had their location identified (<htcite0>), raise no doubts as to their existence, which leads us to a first reflection.

Reflection 1: On facts and poems

How can we differentiate whether something written millennia ago is a historical fact or a poem?

#### Archaeological evidence

The evidence for Shimashki is not abundant, but it exists. Most likely, the rise of the Shimashki dynasty occurred as a result of the fall of Awan (<htcite0>).

However, the few available pieces of evidence are considered historical facts, such as the battle between Ibbi-Sin, king of Ur, and Lurak-luhhan, king of Shimashki, in the year <htdate0> (<htcite1>; <htcite2>).

#### Spelling

In this text, we will work with the Shimashki dynasty (<htcite3>; <htcite4>; <htcite5>). However, in one of the works we are studying, the dynasty is mentioned as *Simashki* (<htcite6>).

Because we have no knowledge of the original language, we adopt the spelling most commonly used by scholars of the region (<htcite7>).

#### Probable location

The possible location of Shimashki is unknown, which does not mean that attempts have not been made to identify it. In his work, Hamidreza Qashqai mentions an initial attempt to locate it in the suburbs of Khorram-Abad, Iran, but this hypothesis was contested, suggesting it might be further west (<htcite0>).

Among the proposals, the hypothesis has also been raised that Anshan was the capital of Shimashki. On the other hand, some consider that it could be north of Anshan (<htcite1>).

What is not in doubt, however, is its location in the present-day territory of Iran.

prerequisites: Before continuing with the text, we suggest studying the texts about the Awan dynasty already published on History Tracers (Elamites - Awan Dynasty).

history.text:
In this text, we will deal with the kings listed by Walther Hinz in his work (<htcite0>), which portrays the history of the kings of Shimashki in the period between <htdate0> and <htdate1>.

In addition to the work of Walther Hinz, we will analyze the work of Hamidreza Qashqai (<htcite1>), which presents us with three additional kings. This divergence regarding the number of kings is even explained in the work of Hamidreza Qashqai, which mentions the possibility that the names of the kings are a combination of different lists (<htcite2>).

The conclusions about the period in which he lived and, consequently, about the total number of kings who ruled — in the works we are studying — are divergent.

#### An initial view

Walther Hinz proposes that the first mention of his name occurred in <htdate0>, indicating that his presence in a list of 12 kings of Susa would be incorrect (<htcite0>). Still in his work, it is mentioned that *"Shu-Sin married one of his daughters to the ‘Prince of Anshan"*, it being impossible to determine precisely whether it is a reference to Girnamme or to a vassal (<htcite1>).

The mention of Anshan leads us to an event that would occur more than a millennium later and that deserves a reflection (<htcite2>):

Reflection 2: He mentioned, then...

After the conquest of Babylon, in , a cylinder was produced for Cyrus (Figure 2) with some information, from which we highlight, for this study, the following excerpt ():

...I am Cyrus, king of the universe, the great king, the powerful king, king of Babylon, king of Sumer and Akkad, king of the four quarters of the world, son of Cambyses, the great king,, king of the city of Anshan, grandson of Cyrus, the great king, ki[ng of the ci]ty of Anshan, descendant of Teispes, the great king, king of Anshan, the perpetual seed of kingship, whose reign Bel and Nabu love, and with whose kingship, to their joy, they concern themselves....

With these phrases, let us make some reflections:

Would Cyrus have only mentioned some ancestors because it was impossible to create a list going back to the first kings of Anshan?

Is there any possibility of linking Cyrus to the kings of Anshan and Shimashki?

Before these questions, did you happen to suppose that Cyrus could be related to the ancient kings of Anshan and Shimashki?

Between and , there is an interval of 1,499 years. Assuming that generations occur every 30 years, that means that if Cyrus were to list his ancestors back to that time, his cylinder would have approximately 49 generations. Considering that we are only beginning the study, do you understand that there would be 49 well-documented kings between Cyrus and the kings of Anshan from the time of Girnamme?

Do you think there is a possibility that someone invented names to connect the first Persian emperor, Cyrus, to historical or imaginary kings of the past, with the aim of creating an identity for him?

If someone had created that list, would you accept it? Would you say it is wrong without researching? Or would you look for all the kings to verify what is fact and what is imaginary?

Since Cyrus was the “king of the universe”, why did he only mention nearby regions in the sequence? Would it not be better to mention all the regions of the world? Or even other planets, to leave no doubt?
Figure 2: Cyrus Cylinder. © Trustees of the British Museum (The British Museum ).

An analysis of the work of Walther Hinz (<htcite0>) shows that the supposed list mentioned in the reflection does not exist. Furthermore, with the current knowledge about Elam, it is not possible to trace a continuous history with the necessary details to identify all of Cyrus's ancestors in that time interval, even though he dominated an extensive empire for his time.

#### The other side

When analyzing the work of Hamidreza Qashqai, we find another proposal. According to him, King Girnamme would have lived around <htdate0> and, in that period, would have obtained dominion over Anshan and Shimashki, which would justify the appearance of his name in the list of kings of Awan (<htcite1>).
Due to the fact that a son is attributed to the king, this person probably must have existed (<htcite0>).
Hypothesis raised in a work analyzed by Hamidreza Qashqai (<htcite0>).
Except for the work of Hamidreza Qashqai, which mentions an ancient tablet referring to kings of Shimashki (<htcite0>), nothing has been found about this supposed king.
Hypothesis raised in a work analyzed by Hamidreza Qashqai (<htcite0>).
Hypothesis raised in a work analyzed by Hamidreza Qashqai (<htcite0>).

The association with his supposed father (<htcite0>) cannot be proven. Furthermore, his very existence could not be confirmed until the discovery of the Igibuni archives (<htcite1>). Now, with the historical confirmation of his existence, we can better understand this king and his influence on the fall of Ur III.

Ebarti, also known as Ebarat, was possibly the king who conquered Susa between the third and ninth years of the reign of Ibbi-Sin of Ur, a city that had previously dominated the region (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

His reign and dominion over Susa were brief; however, years later, his descendants would regain control of the region (<htcite4>; <htcite5>).
Since Ebarti had at least one historically proven child (<htcite0>; <htcite1>), this person must have existed.
According to a hypothesis (<htcite0>).
According to texts from Susa (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

Kindattu does not appear in the sequence of his father, the fourth king, in the Shimashki king list. He is mentioned as the sixth king on that list (<htcite0>).

Kindattu played an important role in the fall of Ur III, in <htdate0>, as shown by a quotation found in the time of Ishbi-Erra (*ki-in-da-du lú elam*) and, later, another that mentions *"the Elamite who was dwelling in Ur"* (<htcite1>).

Initially, Kindattu conquered part of the west and central region of present-day Iran for his domains. However, his greatest conquest would occur later, when he took control of Ur (<htcite2>).
Since Kindattu had at least one historically proven child (<htcite0>; <htcite1>), this person must have existed.
According to texts from Susa (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

Idattu, also mentioned in literature as *Indattu-Inshushinak*, assumed the kingdom around <htdate0> (<htcite0>).

His domains can be measured by the different epithets mentioned in texts from Susa and Mesopotamia. According to these, he would have been king of Shimashki, Elam, Anshan, and prince of Susa (<htcite1>).

In addition to the epithets, alliances stand out, such as the one made with the king of Eshnuna, which resulted in the marriage of his son, Tan-ruhuratir, to Bilalama, the king's daughter (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

Obviously, the kings of Shimashki did not live by victories alone. As Hamidreza Qashqai points out, the Elamites would have been expelled from Ur and southern Babylonia. Despite this, years later they would be controlling all the known lands of Iran (<htcite4>).

Reflection 3: I only know that I know nothing

Did you know about these Elamite domains over Ur, through the kings of Shimashki?

Did you know the Xia dynasty (夏代) of China (Zhongguo 中國), contemporary with these kings ()?

Did you know that the Bantu were in full expansion, which would lead them to occupy all of sub-Saharan Africa ()?

Did you know that, in those times, completely independently, civilizations already existed in the sacred city of Caral, in Peru, and in Egypt (; )?

Suppose a person has no knowledge of anything asked in this reflection so far. Would they be able to explain each of these cultures and civilizations, as well as how humanity moved before this period to build these and other cultures?
The fact that Kindattu had at least one son (<htcite0>; <htcite1>) confirms the existence of this person.
Mentioned in ancient texts together with his father (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).
Mentioned in one of the works studied (<htcite0>).

Tan-ruhuratir, also mentioned in the literature as *Tan-Ruhurater* (<htcite0>), was initially appointed by his father as governor of Susa (<htcite1>).

Tan-ruhuratir participated in his father's alliance politics by marrying Mekubi, daughter of Prince Bilalama of Eshnunna in Babylonia. From this marriage, his successor would have been born (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

Like his father, he is also mentioned with different epithets, among which we highlight “King of Shimashki and Elam,” indicating that he still controlled the region (<htcite4>).

He is also mentioned on a tablet from Susa that most likely does not date from the Ur III period (<htcite5>)
According to the studied works (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).
Although there is no doubt regarding the paternity, only one studied work mentions his mother (<htcite0>).

He possibly had a short reign, lasting about 3 years, with indications that this occurred around <htdate0> (<htcite0>).

Since he was not the first king with the name Idattu, it is not possible to say with certainty whether some epithets or references refer to him or to the first king who used that name (<htcite1>; <htcite2>).

With the data we have so far, we can state that this was the last king of the dynasty.
In this excerpt, we list the kings from the Shimashki list for whom there is no direct evidence of association with the first family studied in this text.

Aside from their mention in the list of the kings of Shimashki as “Man of Anshan” (<htcite0>), nothing else has been found about them.

Hamidreza Qashqai assumes that he could have been the son of Ebarti, since he ruled right after him (<htcite1>).

Lurak-luhhan is identified as the successor of Girnamme by Walther Hinz (<htcite0>); however, as we have seen, recent discoveries have modified this understanding.

On the other hand, the fact that he started wars against Ur during the reign of Ibbi-Sin, just a few years after the latter ascended to the throne, is unquestionable. In those battles, he freed the cities of Awan, Adamdun, and Susa from Sumerian rule. However, years later, he was captured and taken to Ur in subsequent conflicts (<htcite1>; <htcite2>).

Regarding his parentage, it is not possible to say with certainty, with the hypothesis being that he is the son of Tazitta or even of Ebarti (<htcite3>).

Some older publications that studied Shimashki ended up confusing him with another Ebarti already mentioned in this text (<htcite0>). In the literature, the standard was adopted to name this one as Ebarti/Ebarat II, while the previous king came to be called Ebarti/Ebarat I. In this text, we will follow the way the Sumerians mentioned their rulers, without any suffix (**Figure 3**).
Figure 3: Foundation spout with inscription.. © Musée du Louvre ().
**Figure 3** presents an inscription from the period when the temple of Nanna was built, which tells us (<htcite0>; <htcite1>):

• "Ebarat (being) king of Anshan and Susa, Shilhaha (being) sukkal-mah and father-king of the land of Anshan and Susa, Atta-hushu, sukkal and ippir (magistrate?) of Susa, son of the sister-wife of Shilhaha, built the temple of Nanna."

Besides the spelling, another important fact can be observed in **Figure 3**. In it, we have the mention of Ebarat as "king of Anshan and Susa", but not only that. Also mentioned are Shilhaha and Atta-hushu, who, although not listed in the kings' list of Shimashki, could belong to the same family as Ebarat (<htcite0>).

Still regarding Ebarat, it should be emphasized that he is considered the founder of the Sukkalmahat dynasty, with the beginning of his reign around <htdate0> (<htcite1>).

Reflection 4: Shares the same name, therefore...

King Ebarti shares the same name as another king mentioned in the first part of this text. However, there is no tablet that allows us to associate him with that king. Given this scenario:

Would you consider him as belonging to the same previous family?

How would you justify the fact that he is considered the founder of a new dynasty, the Sukkalmahat ()?
Idattu-napir is mentioned alongside Atta-hushu, indicating that the kings were contemporaries and possibly ruled different regions of Elam (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

Reflection 5: Wishes and facts...

In Reflection 2, we mentioned that Cyrus proclaimed himself as a descendant of the kings of Anshan and therefore of Elamite origin, a fact confirmed by other inscriptions (; ).

Darius, when dealing with his lineage in the Behistun inscription (Figure 4) — a lineage that places him, like Cyrus, as a descendant of the same person —, reduces the importance of the Elamite heritage, emphasizing the "Achaemenids" (). Does the shift in narrative focus remove the fact that Anshan and Elam were ancient civilizations and exerted dominance in the region?

Going back more than a millennium to the kings of Shimashki we are studying, we see that the list of kings with unknown fathers is growing. Could these kings have had more difficulty proving their lineage?

Idattu-napir does not appear linked to his father — possibly a king — despite appearing on a list of kings. Millennia later, Darius presented a royal connection that adds to Cyrus an ancestor that Cyrus himself does not mention. Could there be any possible connection between these two scenarios?
Figure 4: Behistun Inscription. Photo taken by Babak Sedighi and made available on the UNESCO website (). Shared under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0 IGO license.
He was mentioned in the list of kings of Shimashki (<htcite0>; <htcite1>). Nothing more than that can we say about him.

exercise_v2.question: Número de gerações antigas são praticamente indeterminadas?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: A medida que retrocedemos no tempo, devido a falta de evidências, torna-se impraticável determinar com precisão o número de gerações. Isto poderia inclusive acabar levando autores tardios a tentarem conexões através de criação de personagens, a fim de completar as lacunas.

exercise_v2.question: O rei Ebarti, que conquistou Susa, foi comprovado recentemente?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Apesar de possuir seu nome em uma lista milenar, a aceitação como figura histórica ocorreu recentemente com descoberta de novos textos que o mencionavam e o posicionavam no período de Ur III.

exercise_v2.question: Apesar de termos passado superficialmente sobre as possíveis relações de Ciro e Dario mais de um milênio após as personagens deste texto terem vivido, ela tem algo em comum com este texto. Não há provas de ligações para todas as pessoas. É possível que textos antigos inventem conexões genealógicas?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: A separação entre o que é história do que é um poema ou desejo de pessoas passa justamente pela existência de provas de existências de pessoas. Fabricação de linhagens não é algo incomum,  muito pelo contrário. Por este motivo, necessitamos estudar todos os documentos e tabletes contemporâneos disponíveis para assegurar que ligações realmente existiram.
